items.question_text: Mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima require direct pilot/controller voice communications. Which of the following satisfies this communications requirement?
items.explanation: The rule requires direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. A controller speaking directly with the pilot on a VHF frequency satisfies the requirement. Relayed communications, regardless of the relay method, do not. The controlling principle is that the communications path must be direct — no intermediary.

choices.text: The pilot communicates through a third aircraft relaying on the same frequency.
The controller and pilot are in direct VHF voice communication.
The pilot communicates through company dispatch, which relays to the controller.
The controller receives position data via datalink while voice is on a separate relay frequency.

items.question_text: An aircraft was estimated over a fix at 1432Z. The position report affecting separation is overdue. By what time must the controller take action to obtain the report?
items.explanation: The 5-minute deadline runs from the estimate time. 1432Z + 5 minutes = 1437Z. Action must be taken no later than 1437Z. The controlling principle is that the clock starts at the estimate time, not when the controller notices the omission.

choices.text: 1442Z, allowing 5 minutes from the time the controller would reasonably notice.
1432Z; action must be immediate upon the estimate time.
No fixed deadline applies when only one report is missed.

items.question_text: Which statement about duplicate position reports is operationally correct?
items.explanation: The rule is an absolute prohibition: an aircraft must not be required to make the same position report to more than one facility. Facility type, operational convenience, and whether one facility heard the report to another are all irrelevant.

choices.text: An aircraft may be required to make the same position report to a tower and a center if both need the information.
An aircraft must not be required to make the same position report to more than one facility.
Duplicate position reports are permitted when the two facilities coordinate the request in advance.
The prohibition applies only when both facilities are of the same type, such as two towers.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly pairs a terminal adjacent-airport wake turbulence category combination with its required separation?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a3 specifies 2 minutes for Category E, F, G, H, or I behind Category C. The other options mismatch the required values: Category F behind A requires 3 minutes (not 2), Category I behind E requires 2 minutes (not 3), and Category D behind B requires 2 minutes (not 3).

choices.text: Category F behind Category A — 2 minutes.
Category H behind Category C — 2 minutes.
Category I behind Category E — 3 minutes.
Category D behind Category B — 3 minutes.

items.question_text: In en route arrival operations, a small aircraft behind a B757 requires 3 minutes when landing to the same runway but only 2 minutes when landing to a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet. Why does the same-runway case require more time?
items.explanation: When landing to the same runway, the trailing aircraft flies directly through the leading aircraft's wake. On a parallel runway (even one separated by less than 2,500 feet), the flight paths diverge, reducing the duration and intensity of wake exposure. This operational distinction explains why the same-runway minima are higher for the B757 case specifically.

choices.text: Aircraft on the same runway fly through the same wake path for a longer duration than on parallel or crossing runways.
The B757 produces more wake on the same runway than on parallel runways.
The same-runway minimum includes an extra coordination minute that the parallel-runway case does not require.
The parallel-runway minimum uses radar miles while the same-runway minimum uses timed minutes.

items.question_text: Which list correctly states the three-tier diverging-course departure minima in order from the smallest to largest separation value?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a lists: (1) immediately after takeoff — 1 minute, (2) within 5 minutes after takeoff — 2 minutes, (3) within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff — 3 miles. Each tier increases the separation value as the time/distance before divergence increases, reflecting longer exposure to potential conflict.

choices.text: 1 minute immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
2 minutes immediately after takeoff; 3 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 5 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
1 minute immediately after takeoff; 3 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 5 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
3 miles immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 1 minute within 13 miles DME/ATD.

items.question_text: For which divergence angle must the preceding aircraft have both passed the runway intersection point AND commenced a turn before the succeeding aircraft may take off from intersecting runways?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c2(b) adds the turn requirement specifically for intersecting runways with 15 to 29 degrees of divergence. For 30 degrees or more under c2(a), only intersection passage is required. The turn ensures positive divergence at shallower angles.

choices.text: 30 degrees or more.
Any divergence angle on intersecting runways.
15 to 29 degrees inclusive.
Less than 15 degrees.

items.question_text: For nonintersecting runways, a divergence of less than 15 degrees requires a centerline distance of at least _____ feet for simultaneous takeoffs.
items.explanation: Subparagraph c1(b)(2) requires at least 3,500 feet when the runways diverge by less than 15 degrees. Compare with c1(b)(1): 2,000 feet for 15-29 degrees. The smaller the divergence, the greater the centerline separation required.

items.question_text: The 3-minute same-course departure minimum applies only when what specific condition is met?
items.explanation: The rule applies when the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft. If the following aircraft will level off below the leading aircraft's altitude, the trigger condition is absent and this rule does not apply. The controlling principle is that the climb-through condition defines the rule's scope.

choices.text: The following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft.
Both aircraft depart from the same runway.
The aircraft are on courses diverging by 45 degrees or more.
The following aircraft is faster than the leading aircraft.

items.question_text: For mixed DME/ATD aircraft pairs on the same course, the DME aircraft must be either at or below _____ feet or outside of _____ miles from the DME NAVAID to use the 5-mile minimum.
items.explanation: The rule requires the DME aircraft to be at or below 10,000 feet, or outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID. Either condition satisfies the qualification — they are alternatives, not cumulative requirements.

items.question_text: In terminal operations with a valid fix at 6 miles, the takeoff direction is aligned with the final approach course reciprocal (differing by less than 45 degrees). What must the departing aircraft accomplish before the arriving aircraft reaches the fix?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b applies when the takeoff direction is other than in subparagraph a. It requires the departing aircraft to be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course before the arrival leaves the fix. This is stricter than subparagraph a, which only requires takeoff to have occurred.

choices.text: Be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course.
Have taken off before the arriving aircraft leaves the fix.
Have reached 4 miles from the airport.
Be at or above the arriving aircraft's altitude before reaching the fix.

items.question_text: Which is the correct phraseology for requiring an aircraft to cross a fix no earlier than a specified time?
items.explanation: The published phraseology provides two formats: CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time) sets a deadline, CROSS (fix) AT OR AFTER (time) sets an earliest crossing time. For 'no earlier than,' AT OR AFTER is correct.

choices.text: CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time).
CROSS (fix) AT OR AFTER (time).
PROCEED TO (fix), ARRIVE NO EARLIER THAN (time).
CROSS (fix) NOT BEFORE (time).

items.question_text: Which speed advantage from the leading aircraft, combined with DME/ATD equipment, authorizes a 5-mile longitudinal separation minimum on same, converging, or crossing courses?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a requires the leading aircraft to maintain a speed at least 44 knots faster for the 5-mile tier (or 3 minutes for non-DME). Subparagraph b requires at least 22 knots for the 10-mile tier (or 5 minutes for non-DME). The fallback under subparagraph d is 20 miles or 10 minutes when neither speed advantage is met.

choices.text: At least 20 knots faster.
At least 30 knots faster.
At least 22 knots faster.
At least 44 knots faster.

items.question_text: For non-DME aircraft climbing or descending through another aircraft's altitude, the 5-minute longitudinal minimum requires all of three conditions. Which condition concerns the maximum permitted vertical distance at the start of the altitude change?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c2(b) requires the aircraft to be separated by not more than 4,000 feet when the altitude change started. The other two conditions are (a) descending leads or climbing follows, and (c) the change starts within 10 minutes after the following aircraft reports over a fix reported by the leading aircraft or acknowledges a crossing-time clearance.

choices.text: Not more than 2,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 4,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 5,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 10,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.

items.question_text: Opposite-course aircraft must be assigned different altitudes from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after they are estimated to pass. This creates a total protected window of _____ minutes.
items.explanation: The rule specifies a 20-minute total window: 10 minutes before plus 10 minutes after estimated passing equals 20 minutes. Vertical separation may be discontinued sooner if one of the listed conditions is met.

items.question_text: Two aircraft on opposite courses have reported passing the same intersection and are 4 minutes apart. May vertical separation be discontinued?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b permits discontinuing vertical separation when both aircraft have reported passing the same intersection or waypoint AND they are at least 3 minutes apart. With 4 minutes, this condition is met. The controlling principle is that a shared reference point plus a minimum time interval confirms the aircraft have passed each other.

choices.text: Yes, because they reported passing the same intersection and are at least 3 minutes apart.
No, because they must report passing different NAVAIDs indicating they have passed each other.
No, because vertical separation must continue for the full 10 minutes after estimated passing.
Yes, but only if they are also at least 8 miles apart.

items.question_text: Two RNAV aircraft on opposite courses report passing the same position. The route is greater than 8 miles in width and the segment requires extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles. What minimum distance must separate the aircraft?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c provides three RNAV distance tiers: 8 miles for routes ≤8 miles wide, 18 miles for routes >8 miles wide, and 30 miles for any route segment requiring extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles. The extended-distance qualifier overrides the route-width tier and mandates 30 miles.

choices.text: 8 miles because the aircraft reported the same position.
18 miles because the route is greater than 8 miles in width.
30 miles because the segment requires extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles.
3 minutes because time-based separation applies when extended distance limitations are in effect.

items.question_text: Which of the following is an approved method for procedural lateral separation?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists four methods: (a) clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose widths do not overlap, (b) clear aircraft below 18,000 to different geographical locations, (c) clear aircraft to hold over different fixes whose holding pattern airspace does not overlap, and (d) clear departing aircraft to fly specified headings that diverge by at least 45 degrees.

choices.text: Clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose protected airspace widths do not overlap.
Assign different altitudes to aircraft on the same airway.
Require aircraft to report position every 50 miles along the route.
Apply 5 miles of radar separation between aircraft on parallel tracks.

items.question_text: Clear departing aircraft to fly specified headings that diverge by at least _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Subparagraph d requires headings that diverge by at least 45 degrees for lateral separation of departing aircraft. This is the same 45-degree threshold used in 6-2-1 for diverging-course departure separation.

items.question_text: The diverging-radials lateral separation procedure requires radials of the same NAVAID to diverge by at least _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 requires at least 15 degrees of divergence. This is the minimum threshold; below this angle the procedure does not apply regardless of aircraft distance from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: The non-DME diverging-radials table specifies a required distance for each divergence angle. When the actual divergence angle falls between two values in the table, which value is used?
items.explanation: When the divergence angle falls between two table values, the next higher distance value is used. This conservative approach ensures separation is never understated when the exact angle does not match a table entry.

choices.text: The next lower value in the table.
The average of the two adjacent values.
The next higher value in the table.
The value for the closest whole-degree entry.

items.question_text: Between different DME arcs about the same NAVAID at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID, the minimum lateral separation is _____ miles.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 specifies 10 miles for arc-to-arc separation at 35 miles or less. At more than 35 miles, subparagraph a2 increases this to 20 miles. The doubling reflects greater position uncertainty farther from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: A controller assigns one aircraft to fly a 30-mile DME arc about a VOR, and needs to protect other airspace inside that arc. The airspace to be protected is within 35 miles of the NAVAID. What minimum must be maintained between the arc and that airspace?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b1 specifies 5 miles between an arc and other airspace to be protected at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID. This is half the arc-to-arc minimum for the same distance range, reflecting that one aircraft is on a defined arc while the other is in a designated airspace volume.

choices.text: 5 miles.
10 miles, the same as arc-to-arc separation.
20 miles because the arc is at 30 miles from the NAVAID.
No minimum applies because the airspace is inside the arc.

items.question_text: For a course change of 75 degrees at FL200, the overflown-side protection is 14 miles. For a course change of 120 degrees at the same altitude, the protection is 28 miles. What general principle explains this difference?
items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c establish two angle ranges (16-90 and 91-180 degrees) with different protection values. Larger turns sweep through more airspace on the overflown side, requiring more protection. At FL180-230, the values are 14 miles (16-90°) and 28 miles (91-180°). The controlling principle is geometric: a wider turn arc covers more area before the aircraft establishes on the new course.

choices.text: Larger course changes sweep through more airspace on the overflown side before the aircraft establishes on the new course.
The 91-180 degree range always requires exactly double the protection because aircraft accelerate during sharp turns.
The 28-mile value includes extra buffer for wake turbulence that only appears during turns greater than 90 degrees.
Higher-altitude turns require more protection regardless of the turn angle.

items.question_text: For RNAV aircraft on diverging or crossing courses, lateral separation exists when one aircraft is beyond the point where protected airspaces cease to overlap. On a route greater than 8 miles in width where extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles are required, this distance is _____ miles.
items.explanation: Subparagraph b specifies 9 miles for routes >8 miles wide, except 15 miles when extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles apply. The extended-distance qualifier overrides the standard route-width tier.

items.question_text: What is the required non-overlap distance for RNAV aircraft on a route that is 8 miles or less in width?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a specifies 4 miles for routes 8 miles or less in width. This is the smallest non-overlap distance in the RNAV diverging/crossing table, reflecting the narrower protected airspace.

choices.text: 4 miles.
9 miles.
8 miles.
15 miles.

items.question_text: REPORT LEAVING ODD/EVEN ALTITUDES/FLIGHT LEVELS.
items.explanation: This is the published phraseology for requesting reports at each odd or even altitude change during climb or descent. It is distinct from 'REPORT LEAVING/REACHING (altitude/flight level)' which requests a single report at one specific altitude.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When severe turbulence is reported, what must an aircraft previously at an altitude report before that altitude can be assigned to another aircraft?
items.explanation: The exception at subparagraph a triggers the stricter rule: the aircraft must report at or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minimum. This is a more demanding requirement than the basic rule (merely report leaving), reflecting the increased risk of altitude deviations in severe turbulence.

choices.text: That it has left the altitude.
At or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minimum.
That severe turbulence has subsided.
That it is ready for the next altitude assignment.

items.question_text: An aircraft on a cruise clearance verbally reports leaving 9,000 feet in descent. May the pilot later return to 9,000 feet without a new clearance?
items.explanation: The note states that when a pilot on a cruise clearance verbally reports leaving an altitude in descent, the pilot may not return to that altitude. The cruise clearance gives a block of airspace, but the verbal descent report permanently surrenders the higher portions of that block.

choices.text: Yes, because the cruise clearance assigns a block of airspace up to the cruising altitude.
No, because the verbal report of leaving 9,000 feet in descent means the pilot may not return to that altitude.
Yes, provided the controller confirms via Mode C that 9,000 feet is unoccupied.
No, because a cruise clearance prohibits any altitude changes once a descent has begun.

items.question_text: During pilot-maintained vertical separation, which aircraft is authorized to maintain separation during a climb, and which during a descent?
items.explanation: The rule authorizes the lower aircraft if climbing, or the upper aircraft if descending. The aircraft whose maneuver closes the vertical distance is the one responsible for maintaining separation — the climbing lower aircraft closes from below, the descending upper aircraft closes from above.

choices.text: The higher aircraft in both cases.
The lower aircraft during climb, the upper aircraft during descent.
The lower aircraft in both cases.
Both aircraft must maintain separation simultaneously.

items.question_text: When only one missed approach procedure is available for timed approaches, the existing ceiling must be at or above the highest _____ minimum established for the airport.
items.explanation: The rule requires the ceiling to be at or above the highest circling minimum. This ensures that an aircraft executing the sole missed approach can safely transition to a circling maneuver if needed.

choices.text: circling

items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies the elements that must be included when clearing the succeeding aircraft for a timed approach?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a requires: the approach clearance, the time to leave the approach fix inbound, and the time to start the approach procedure turn if applicable. These three elements give the pilot complete timing information for the sequence.

choices.text: Approach clearance, time to leave the approach fix inbound, and time to start procedure turn if applicable.
Approach clearance, assigned altitude, and missed approach procedure.
Time to leave the approach fix, radar vector heading, and frequency change.
Approach clearance, release to tower, and landing runway assignment.

items.question_text: During a timed-approach sequence, at what point must the controller release the succeeding aircraft to the tower?
items.explanation: The rule requires the aircraft to be released to the tower before it reaches the final approach fix. This ensures the tower has positive control during the final approach segment.

choices.text: Before the aircraft reaches the final approach fix.
After the aircraft reports established on the final approach course.
Before the aircraft begins the procedure turn.
After the preceding aircraft has landed and cleared the runway.

items.question_text: The standard minimum interval between successive timed approaches is _____ minutes or a _____-mile radar interval. Larger intervals apply for wake turbulence situations specified in subparagraphs b and c.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a specifies 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval as the standard minimum. Wake turbulence exceptions in subparagraphs b and c may increase these values for specific aircraft pairs.

items.question_text: In en route timed approaches, a small aircraft behind a heavy aircraft requires what wake turbulence interval?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b2 specifies small behind heavy: 3 minutes or 6 miles. This is the same interval as heavy behind super (b1(a)), reflecting similar wake vulnerability.

choices.text: 3 minutes or 6 miles.
4 minutes or 8 miles.
3 minutes or 7 miles.
2 minutes or 5 miles.

items.question_text: An aircraft on a timed approach misses. Under what condition may the next aircraft continue its approach?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b permits the next aircraft to continue if it has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure. Remaining aircraft must be held or retained under radar control until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.

choices.text: Only if the next aircraft has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure.
Only if the preceding aircraft reports clear of the missed approach path.
Only if the weather is at or above VFR minimums.
The next aircraft must always discontinue the approach and hold after any missed approach.

items.instruction: Which communications scenario permits use of mileage-based separation?
items.question_text: In which of the following situations may the controller apply mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima?

choices.text: The pilot communicates through a relay aircraft on the same frequency.
The pilot and controller are in direct VHF voice communication.
The pilot sends position reports via datalink while a third party relays voice.
The pilot communicates through company dispatch which relays to ATC.

items.explanation: The rule requires direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. Direct communication means the controller and pilot speak to each other without an intermediary. Relayed communications, regardless of the relay method, do not satisfy this requirement.

items.instruction: For each missed position report, determine whether the 5-minute action rule applies.
items.question_text: Three aircraft miss position reports. Aircraft A misses a report at a fix with no known traffic within 50 miles. Aircraft B misses a report at a fix where another IFR aircraft is on a converging course 8 minutes away. Aircraft C misses a report at a fix where the controller is spacing it behind another aircraft on the same airway. Which missed reports trigger the mandatory action requirement?

choices.text: Aircraft A and C only.
Aircraft B and C only.
All three aircraft trigger the requirement.
Aircraft A only.

items.explanation: The rule applies to position reports affecting separation. Aircraft B's report affects separation with the converging aircraft. Aircraft C's report affects spacing. Aircraft A's report does not affect separation and therefore does not trigger the rule.

items.instruction: Identify which situation violates the duplicate position report rule.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is the duplicate position report rule being violated?

choices.text: The center asks a pilot to report a fix, and the tower independently asks the same pilot to report the same fix — neither facility coordinates with the other.
The tower asks a pilot to report a fix that the center also requested, but the facilities coordinated the request beforehand.
The center asks a pilot to report five different fixes along the route to provide periodic position updates.
The tower asks a pilot to report the same fix to two different tower controllers working different positions.

items.explanation: The rule prohibits requiring an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility. The first option describes two different facilities independently requiring the same report — the prohibited conduct. Multiple reports to controllers within the same facility is not a violation of this rule.

items.instruction: For each category pair, identify which requires 3 minutes of wake turbulence separation in terminal adjacent-airport operations.
items.question_text: In terminal adjacent-airport operations, which of the following category pairs requires 3 minutes rather than 2 minutes of wake turbulence separation?

choices.text: Category D behind Category B.
Category H behind Category C.
Category F behind Category A.
Category I behind Category E.

items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 specifies 3 minutes for any Category B through I behind Category A. All other terminal adjacent-airport pairs use 2 minutes: Category B-I behind B or D (a2), Category E-I behind C (a3), and Category I behind E (a4).

items.instruction: Compare en route arrival minima for same-runway versus parallel/crossing-runway operations for a small aircraft behind a B757.
items.question_text: In en route arrival operations, a small aircraft behind a B757 requires 3 minutes to the same runway but only 2 minutes to a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet. What operational factor explains the difference?

choices.text: Same-runway arrivals fly through the same wake path for longer, while parallel-runway flight paths diverge, reducing wake exposure time.
The B757 produces more wake on the same runway than when landing on a parallel runway.
Parallel-runway arrivals always use radar separation as the primary method.
The same-runway minimum includes an extra minute for coordination between approach and tower.

items.explanation: When aircraft land on the same runway, the trailing aircraft flies directly through the leading aircraft's wake. On parallel runways, the flight paths diverge, reducing the duration of wake exposure. This reduced risk permits a smaller minimum for the parallel/crossing case, but only for the B757 — super and heavy lead aircraft have the same minima in both cases.

items.instruction: Arrange the three diverging-course departure minima in order from smallest to largest separation value.
items.question_text: Which list correctly orders the three diverging-course departure minima from the smallest separation value to the largest?

choices.text: 1 minute immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 1 minute immediately after takeoff.
2 minutes within 5 minutes; 3 minutes immediately; 5 miles within 13 miles.
1 minute immediately; 3 minutes within 5 minutes; 5 miles within 13 miles.

items.explanation: The three-tier progression is: immediately after takeoff → 1 minute; within 5 minutes → 2 minutes; within 13 miles DME/ATD → 3 miles. Each tier uses a larger value because divergence occurs later, increasing the period during which aircraft are on potentially conflicting paths.

items.instruction: Determine whether the 5-mile DME/ATD minimum may be applied in each mixed-equipment scenario. The DME aircraft must be either at or below 10,000 feet OR outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID.
items.question_text: A controller has three aircraft pairs on the same course. Pair 1: DME aircraft at 9,000 feet and 5 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. Pair 2: DME aircraft at 15,000 feet and 15 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. Pair 3: DME aircraft at 12,000 feet and 8 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. For which pairs is the 5-mile same-course minimum authorized?

choices.text: Pairs 1 and 2 only.
All three pairs.
Pair 3 only.
Pair 1 only.

items.explanation: For mixed DME/ATD use, the DME aircraft qualifies if EITHER at/below 10,000 feet OR outside 10 miles. Pair 1: 9,000 feet satisfies the altitude prong. Pair 2: 15 miles satisfies the distance prong. Pair 3: 12,000 feet (above 10,000) AND 8 miles (inside 10) fails both prongs.

items.instruction: Compare the fix-based and time-based departure/arrival separation methods.
items.question_text: When the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course, the fix-based method has the departure take off before the arrival leaves a fix at least 4 miles from the airport. The time-based method has the departure take off 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport. Why is the fix-based method preferred when an appropriate fix exists?

choices.text: The fix-based method uses a precise geographic reference rather than an estimate, reducing position uncertainty.
The fix-based method always requires less time between the departure and arrival.
The time-based method requires DME equipment that is not available at all airports.
The fix-based method eliminates the need for the 45-degree divergence requirement.

items.explanation: The fix-based method uses an actual geographic reference — the aircraft's position relative to a known fix — which is more precise than an estimated arrival time. Subparagraph c makes the time-based method the fallback, reinforcing that fix-based is the preferred method.

items.instruction: Construct the correct phraseology for a fix-crossing deadline.
items.question_text: A controller needs an aircraft to cross FIXXX no later than 1430Z. Which phraseology correctly conveys this restriction?

choices.text: CROSS FIXXX AT OR BEFORE 1430Z.
CROSS FIXXX NO LATER THAN 1430Z.
CROSS FIXXX AT OR AFTER 1430Z.
PROCEED TO FIXXX, ARRIVE BY 1430Z.

items.explanation: The published phraseology is 'CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time).' This is the prescribed wording for a fix-crossing time restriction. 'AT OR AFTER' serves the opposite purpose (earliest crossing time). Nonstandard variations are not the authorized phraseology.

items.instruction: Match each speed advantage to the correct longitudinal separation minimum for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft.
items.question_text: Match each leading-aircraft speed advantage to the correct longitudinal separation minimum for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft: (1) 50 knots faster; (2) 30 knots faster; (3) 15 knots faster.

choices.text: (1) 5 miles; (2) 10 miles; (3) 20 miles.
(1) 10 miles; (2) 20 miles; (3) 30 miles.
(1) 5 miles; (2) 5 miles; (3) 10 miles.
(1) 3 minutes; (2) 5 minutes; (3) 10 minutes.

items.explanation: The rule provides three tiers: ≥44 knots → 5 miles (a), ≥22 knots → 10 miles (b), and neither → 20 miles fallback (d). 50 knots qualifies for the 5-mile tier, 30 knots for the 10-mile tier (≥22 but <44), and 15 knots qualifies for neither, requiring the 20-mile fallback.

items.instruction: Determine which opposite-course vertical separation discontinuation method is satisfied in each situation.
items.question_text: For which of the following situations may vertical separation on opposite courses be discontinued?

choices.text: Both aircraft report passing the same VOR and are 2 minutes apart.
Two RNAV aircraft on a route 6 miles wide report passing the same position and are 8 miles apart.
Both aircraft report passing NAVAIDs indicating they have passed each other, but only 5 minutes have elapsed since the estimated passing time.
Two RNAV aircraft on a route 12 miles wide report passing the same position and are 15 miles apart.

items.explanation: The second option satisfies subparagraph c: RNAV aircraft on a route ≤8 miles wide need 8 miles. The first fails subparagraph b because 2 min < 3 min. The third fails subparagraph a because the 10-minute window hasn't closed. The fourth fails subparagraph c: >8 miles wide requires 18 miles, and 15 < 18.

items.instruction: The controller issued pilot-maintained longitudinal separation authorization. Evaluate the pilot readback.
items.clearance: MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES OR TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM N123AB.
items.question_text: Controller: "MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES OR TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM N123AB." Which pilot response correctly interprets the instruction?

choices.text: "Wilco, we will maintain at least 10 minutes or 20 miles from N123AB."
"Roger, maintaining 10 miles from N123AB."
"Wilco, we will maintain at least 5 minutes or 10 miles separation from N123AB."
"Roger, we'll maintain at least 20 minutes separation from N123AB."

items.explanation: The phraseology 'ONE ZERO MINUTES/TWO ZERO MILES' means 10 minutes or 20 miles. The pilot correctly reads back both options. The 5-minute/10-mile values are controller-applied longitudinal separation under 6-4-2b, not pilot-maintained separation.

items.instruction: Use the diverging-radials table interpolation rule.
items.question_text: Two aircraft are on radials of the same VOR diverging by 25 degrees. The non-DME divergence table does not list 25 degrees directly — it lists values for 20 degrees and 30 degrees. What interpolation rule applies?

choices.text: Use the next higher value from the table.
Use the next lower value from the table.
Average the values for the two adjacent entries.
Round the divergence angle to the nearest table entry.

items.explanation: When the divergence angle falls between two table values, the rule specifies using the next higher distance value. This is conservative: it ensures separation is never understated when the exact angle doesn't match a table entry.

items.instruction: Construct the correct DME arc assignment phraseology.
items.question_text: A controller assigns an aircraft to fly a 25-mile DME arc south of the ABC VOR. Which phraseology is correct?

choices.text: VIA 25 MILE ARC SOUTH OF ABC VOR.
FLY THE 25 DME ARC SOUTH OF ABC.
PROCEED VIA 25 MILE ARC SOUTH OF ABC.
MAINTAIN 25 MILE DME ARC SOUTH OF ABC.

items.explanation: The published phraseology is 'VIA (number of miles) MILE ARC (direction) OF (name of DME NAVAID).' Deviations from this format are not the authorized phraseology.

items.instruction: Compare overflown-side protection for different course change angles.
items.question_text: At FL200, the overflown-side protection is 14 miles for a 75-degree turn but 28 miles for a 120-degree turn. Why does the protection double for the larger turn angle?

choices.text: Larger course changes sweep through more airspace on the overflown side before the aircraft establishes on the new course.
The 91-180 degree range always requires exactly double the protection because aircraft accelerate during sharper turns.
The larger value includes a buffer for crossing traffic that only exists during turns greater than 90 degrees.
Protection values are based on turn radius, and a 120-degree turn has exactly double the radius of a 75-degree turn.

items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c establish two angle ranges (16-90° and 91-180°) with different protection values. A larger turn sweeps through a larger area before the aircraft establishes on the new course. At FL180-230, the values are 14 miles (16-90°) and 28 miles (91-180°).

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether the basic rule or the stricter altitude reassignment rule applies.
items.question_text: In which situation must the controller wait until the previous aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum before reassigning the altitude?

choices.text: An aircraft reports leaving FL240 in level flight and the controller wants to assign FL240 to another aircraft.
Severe turbulence has been reported and the controller wants to assign FL240 to another aircraft.
Two aircraft are in level flight vertically separated by 2,000 feet.
An aircraft reports reaching its newly assigned altitude.

items.explanation: The stricter rule applies when any exception is triggered: severe turbulence, military aerial refueling, pilot's-discretion clearance, or cruise clearance. Severe turbulence is one of those exceptions; the basic rule (report leaving) does not apply.

items.instruction: Compare en route and terminal wake turbulence intervals for timed approaches.
items.question_text: In timed approaches, a Category H aircraft behind a Category A aircraft in terminal operations requires 4 minutes or 8 miles. Which en route weight-class pair requires the same interval?

choices.text: Small behind super — 4 minutes or 8 miles.
Small behind heavy — 3 minutes or 6 miles.
Heavy behind super — 3 minutes or 6 miles.
Large behind super — 3 minutes or 7 miles.

items.explanation: Both Category H/I behind A (terminal) and small behind super (en route) represent maximum wake vulnerability — the smallest aircraft behind the largest wake producer — and both require 4 minutes or 8 miles.

items.instruction: An aircraft on a timed approach misses. Identify the correct sequence of controller actions.
items.question_text: An aircraft in a timed-approach sequence misses the approach. The next aircraft has an alternative missed approach procedure. What is the correct sequence of controller actions?

choices.text: Allow the next aircraft to continue; retain radar control or hold remaining aircraft until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.
Hold all aircraft at assigned altitudes, cancel all approach clearances, and restart the sequence.
Clear the next aircraft for a straight-in approach and release remaining aircraft to the tower immediately.
Allow the next aircraft to continue and clear all remaining aircraft for their approaches without delay.

items.explanation: Subparagraph b instructs: (1) allow the next aircraft to continue if it has an alternative missed approach procedure, and (2) retain radar control or hold remaining aircraft until traffic conditions permit. The controller must not release remaining aircraft until the traffic situation resolves.

items.front: From what event does the 5-minute clock start for obtaining a missing position report that affects separation?
items.back: From when the aircraft was estimated over the fix — not from when the controller notices the missing report.

items.front: In terminal adjacent-airport operations, what are the four wake turbulence separation values and their corresponding lead-trail category pairs?
items.back: 3 min — any B-I behind A (a1). 2 min — any B-I behind B or D (a2). 2 min — E-I behind C (a3). 2 min — I behind E (a4).

items.front: For nonintersecting runways in terminal operations, what centerline distance is required for simultaneous takeoffs when the runways diverge by less than 15 degrees?
items.back: At least 3,500 feet between runway centerlines at and beyond the takeoff points.

items.front: What are the two authorized separation methods for same-course departures when the following aircraft will climb through the leading aircraft's altitude?
items.back: 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft; OR 5 miles between DME-equipped aircraft, RNAV-equipped using ATD, or mixed DME/ATD with the DME aircraft at/below 10,000 ft or outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID.

items.front: What two speed advantages trigger reduced longitudinal separation minima, and what are the corresponding minima for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft?
items.back: Leading aircraft at least 44 knots faster → 5 miles. At least 22 knots faster → 10 miles. Neither speed advantage → 20 miles fallback.

items.front: What is the time window during which opposite-course aircraft must be assigned different altitudes?
items.back: From 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after they are estimated to pass.

items.front: What two conditions must be met before a controller may authorize the following aircraft to maintain longitudinal separation by pilot concurrence?
items.back: The pilots must be on the same course and in direct radio communication with each other, and they must concur.

items.front: What is the minimum divergence angle required between radials of the same NAVAID before the diverging-radials lateral separation procedure may be applied?
What are the DME arc minima between different arcs about the same NAVAID?
items.back: At 35 miles or less from the NAVAID: 10 miles. More than 35 miles from the NAVAID: 20 miles.

items.front: For RNAV aircraft on diverging or crossing courses, how far beyond the point where protected airspaces cease to overlap must an aircraft be for lateral separation to exist?
items.back: Route 8 miles or less in width: 4 miles. Route greater than 8 miles: 9 miles, or 15 miles if extended usable distance limitation beyond 130 NM applies.

items.front: What are the four exceptions that require the stricter altitude reassignment rule?
items.back: 1. Severe turbulence is reported. 2. Military aerial refueling. 3. Aircraft issued pilot's-discretion climb/descent. 4. Aircraft on a cruise clearance.

items.front: What triggers the issuance of a clearance for the succeeding aircraft in a timed-approach sequence?
items.back: When the preceding aircraft passes the final approach fix inbound (nonprecision) or the outer marker/fix used in lieu of outer marker inbound (precision).

items.front: Under what condition may the next aircraft continue a timed approach after the preceding aircraft misses?
items.back: Only if it has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure. Otherwise it must be held or kept under radar control until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.
